SpaceX's Starship, the most anticipated vehicle, remains in test flights and faces years of delay before commercial availability due to SpaceX's internal satellite priorities. Blue Origin's New Glenn has yet to demonstrate operational capability after three launch attempts.

Cowboy Space's satellites will weigh 20,000 to 25,000 kilograms each and house approximately 800 GPUs generating 1 megawatt of power. The company's rocket will be slightly more powerful than Falcon 9 but smaller than Starship. Rather than separating the payload from the vehicle, Cowboy Space plans to integrate its data centers directly into the rocket's second stage—a design philosophy that reduces complexity and echoes Explorer 1, the first U.S. satellite.

## Why Vertical Integration Matters Now

For enterprise AI infrastructure planning, this development reflects a hardening constraint. As AI training and inference workloads expand, companies face a choice between accepting terrestrial data center limitations or betting on nascent space infrastructure. Cowboy Space's decision to internalize rocket development acknowledges that the space launch market remains immature and supply-constrained—even as demand accelerates.

The company has recruited experienced talent, including a former Blue Origin propulsion engineer and former SpaceX launch director. It plans to manufacture its own rocket engines, the costliest and most complex component of any launch vehicle. However, rocket development remains exceptionally difficult; most private entrants have failed or stalled. Cowboy Space's advantage lies in narrow focus: it builds only for data center deployment, reducing design complexity compared to general-purpose launchers. For organizations evaluating orbital compute infrastructure, this signals that dedicated launch solutions—not commercial generalists—may be necessary to scale space-based workloads.
items.original_title: There aren’t enough rockets for space data centers. Cowboy Space raised $275 million to build them.

items.headline: Trust and Workflow Design, Not Speed, Drive Enterprise AI Scale
items.body:
Enterprise leaders scaling AI successfully are rejecting the premise that faster deployment equals better outcomes. Research from interviews with executives at Philips, BBVA, Mirakl, Scout24, JetBrains, and Scania reveals a consistent pattern: organizations pulling ahead treat AI as an operating layer embedded in deliberate workflow redesign, not as a feature to be rolled out. The distinction matters because it shifts the burden of success from IT speed to organizational readiness—culture, governance, and quality control.

## Five Patterns From Leading Enterprises

The research identified repeating themes across organizations managing scaled AI deployments. Culture and literacy came before tooling; teams that invested in employee confidence and permission to experiment saw faster adoption than those that led with technology. Governance proved an accelerant rather than a brake—when security, legal, compliance, and IT engaged as design partners early, teams moved faster later with fewer reversals. Ownership emerged as critical: teams that could redesign workflows and build with AI, rather than passively consume it, drove sustainable adoption. Organizations also delayed launches when quality metrics fell short, and the most durable gains came from hybrid workflows that augmented expert reasoning rather than automating it away.

## Where This Takes Enterprise AI

The shift signals a maturation in how organizations think about AI ROI. Rather than measuring success by feature adoption or cost reduction per transaction, leaders are now mapping AI into end-to-end workflows where human oversight remains embedded. This requires defining what "good" looks like before scaling, investing in evaluation frameworks, and treating AI governance as a competitive advantage, not a compliance checkbox.

For enterprises planning scaled deployments, the implication is structural: trust and ownership cannot be retrofitted after rollout. Organizations need to build literacy and judgment-preserving workflows into the design phase, not treat them as post-launch concerns. The practical takeaway is to audit your current readiness across accountability, trust, workflow fit, and quality before expanding AI use beyond pockets of early adoption.
items.original_title: How enterprises are scaling AI
